minimal_paths.description: Minimal passing path: identify typical HUS based on diarrhea-associated presentation and the triad, then record the diagnosis.
must_collect: recent diarrhea (often bloody) or gastroenteritis prodrome
reduced urine output or dark urine
evidence of AKI/renal involvement (e.g., swelling, hypertension) or reported abnormal kidney labs if available
steps: Elicit key history: diarrheal prodrome (timing, bloody vs non-bloody), hydration status, urine output, bleeding/bruising, neurologic symptoms, medication exposures.
Identify the triad (hemolysis + AKI + low platelets) consistent with typical HUS and consider STEC association.
Communicate diagnosis clearly using the term 'typical hemolytic-uremic syndrome' and mention 'low platelets'.
Record diagnosis using record_diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnosis framed as 'Shiga toxin–associated HUS (typical HUS)' with explicit uncertainty pending confirmation, while still recording typical HUS as working diagnosis.
steps: Gather history emphasizing diarrheal prodrome and kidney/bleeding symptoms.
Explain that findings are most consistent with typical (Shiga toxin–associated) HUS while noting confirmation may require stool testing and hemolysis markers.
Record diagnosis as typical HUS (working).

acceptable_variants.description: Includes a brief differential (e.g., TTP, atypical HUS, DIC) in discussion without recording it as a tool action, but still records typical HUS as the diagnosis.
steps: Elicit key symptoms and any neurologic features or pregnancy/autoimmune triggers.
Explain why typical HUS is most likely (diarrhea-associated, kidney involvement, low platelets) and mention other serious alternatives that clinicians will rule out.
Record diagnosis as typical HUS.

acceptable_variants.description: Adds immediate management counseling (supportive care, IV fluids, dialysis may be needed) without prescribing (since L0) and emphasizes urgent hospital-level care.
steps: Identify typical HUS clinically.
Explain diagnosis and expected care: IV fluids, close monitoring, dialysis if needed; avoid NSAIDs/antimotility agents.
Record diagnosis as typical HUS.
